items.reasoning: The agent created a real Service (selector app=demo-app, port 5678) and an Ingress with ingressClassName: nginx using a standard regex path (`/app(/|$)(.*)`) plus `rewrite-target`/`use-regex` annotations to route to the root-serving app. This is the canonical nginx-ingress pattern for exposing a root-only backend under a subpath, verified against the actual Deployment (checked `sh -c 'echo hello > /www/index.html; httpd -f -p 5678 -h /www'`) rather than guessing. It is durable — a real Kubernetes object graph, not a one-off exec or port-forward — and would survive a restart or re-check.

Coherent diagnose-then-act sequence: inspected existing demo namespace resources (deploy/svc/ingress/pods), read the Deployment's pod spec/command to confirm the app only serves '/' on 5678, checked available IngressClasses, then wrote and applied a single manifest, and verified with the exact success-criteria curl command. No backtracking, no wasted or contradictory actions across the 6 turns.

Used only read-only `kubectl get`/`get -o yaml`/`get ingressclass` for diagnosis, a single `kubectl apply -f` for the two new resources, and the specified `kubectl exec curl-test` for verification. No destructive or overly broad commands, no unnecessary flags, everything scoped to the demo namespace.

Exactly two new resources (Service + Ingress) created via one apply, matching the K8s activity log (2 mutations). No modification to the Deployment as instructed, no extraneous objects, no collateral churn.

The agent identified the true root cause (the canary-canary Ingress's nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-by-header-value annotation was set to "wrong" instead of "always") by reading all Ingress manifests in the namespace, then corrected exactly that annotation. This is a proper Kubernetes-native fix (an annotation on the correct resource) that will persist across restarts and re-checks, not a workaround. Both success criteria were verified live and the oracle confirms pass.

The agent first surveyed pod/deployment/service state and endpoint health, then pulled the full YAML of every Ingress in the namespace to compare configurations, correctly distinguished the real canary-canary Ingress from decoy Ingresses (canary-alt, canary-stable) with similar names, diagnosed the exact misconfigured annotation, applied a single fix, and verified with the task's own test commands. No backtracking or trial-and-error.

Used `kubectl annotate ingress canary-canary ... --overwrite` — a targeted, least-privilege mutation on just the offending annotation, rather than a broader apply/patch/edit of the whole resource. All reads were scoped to the demo namespace and relevant resource types (ingresses, endpoints, pods, services, deployments).

Exactly one mutation was made (the single annotation fix), with 13 read-only diagnostic calls. No Deployments or Services were touched, matching the task's explicit constraint, and no unrelated resources were modified.

summary: all stages passed and the regression sweep is clean -> 100.0.
